abstract: Archaeological investigations conducted between 2009 and 2014 in the northern sector of Prague's New Town have significantly advanced our understanding of the largest medieval Jewish necropolis in Bohemia, which served the community from the latter half of the 13th century until the late 15th century. Fieldwork was primarily concentrated around the construction site of the Quadrio multifunctional complex—an area defined by dense urban habitation dating back to the early Middle Ages, which remained largely intact until the development of the Prague Metro in the 1970s. These latest findings regarding the Jewish cemetery provide a vital new layer to the complex settlement mosaic of this historic district, enriching our perspective on the area's long-term topographical evolution.
